dataList.content: First, thread the suture through the eye of the needle.
#27 suturing sinew is a synthetic z-twist fiber assembled by using the ingredient's coracobrachialis muscle and calcaneal tendon. It is to be threaded through the #2 bodkin needle.
Then, suture and close the fascial incision located on the interior.

The feedback received at the Critique session was that the animal in focus is to be made "more obvious." Therefore, parts taken from other animals have been removed wholesale, and additional primary material has been procured to compensate for the missing components.
Now, place a thick patch of reasonably pre-trimmed material over the fascial incision and suture it shut.

Using only the primary ingredient will fall short of the tensility goal established when my original blueprint was conceived, which was designed with access to a wider variety of ingredients in mind; still, this technique will allow me to achieve a similar effect.
The current objective remains the same.
Receive an evaluation higher than a B- and attain a measurable improvement beyond the status quo.

Because it is customary for individuals who have chosen to join this collective to engage themselves with a consistent output schedule and steady improvement in evaluations, further adjustments to this objective seem unnecessary.

The Docent has advised that I could perhaps use the desire to coil the ring twice, thrice around my ring finger as fuel to "obsess" myself with completing the work.
However, I have no desires of the kind.

Other points of feedback from the Docent were likewise executed without issue. I have watched 62 videos of dog fighting pits, paid 7 visits to the Fauvist animal conservatory, watched 71 documentaries on predatory animals, and spent 284 hours living alongside the primary material candidate. However, none of these advised activities has led to observable improvements in the evaluations of my work.
The purpose of this assignment is to "feel" what it is to be <ruby=wild, beastly>Fauve</ruby>, to be impulsive in a way that only animals can.

However, as the instruction manual did not include any references to how the aforementioned "feel" could be applied to artwork, I have been unable to identify a measurable means by which it may be translated to the physical medium.
I have punctured the transfusion bag. Blood prepared for curatorial staging is leaking out.
Revisiting these memories must have made me somewhat emotional—the touch of my fingertips has become too rough.

In such a case, temporarily seal the perforation with a large gauze sheet. According to instructions, materials with this level of thinness require #21 ultrafine needles and 6-times-delaminated nerve fiber thread to handle.
... This fiber is too brittle.
Experience dictates that employing the #18 bone needle with myofiber thread would be most effective for this task.
I will continue to attempt the current method nonetheless, as I must work in accordance with the instruction manual.
This work will require a longer time investment than initially calculated.

Enough blood has now leaked to drench the material's auburn fur. While this has the potential to partially constitute an expression of "le Fauve," the instruction manual did not state that products of happenstance can be used for this assignment. As such, the blood will be removed.
... Perhaps the veracity of these instructions is to be doubted and reviewed.
The concept of "le Fauve," conceived as a product of my experiences, does not seem to agree with what is conveyed by the instruction manual.
Because what is more fauve than the crimson that so insidiously encroaches upon the brownish pelt like that of a wild hound that has just torn open its prey's jugular?
Yet...
... how much faith can one put in what their gaze has beheld?
There is no empirical method by which this perception can be impartially confirmed.
Then...
... judgment will be reserved.
My ability to patch the perforation on the transfusion bag before the complete loss of blood is limited by my insufficient skill.
Which means that... more of the ingredient must be gathered.
This artwork's aesthetic qualities have not met the standards, but its functional merits have been field-tested and proven.

Its claws extend near-instantaneously from their retracted position when the starter cord is pulled—it is highly functional. Each claw on this work has a sharp edge, making it a most suitable tool for harvesting ingredients.

The artwork's inner lining is a subcutaneous fatty layer that has been rearranged for a comfortable fit when equipped over an arm, and beneath the enveloping fur is an enclosure made from mesentery to minimize damage to its interior.
My submissions have consistently scored a series of B-, which can evince that there is a degree of objectivity to my aforementioned assessment.
... However...
... I have been unable to address the feedback that my work "lacks punch," which is an evaluation that has little to do with the artwork's functionalities.
Just what am I supposed to—
Mm.
Injection needles inside the helmet have been activated.
The paint enters my bloodstream, reinforcing the connection between me and my work,
and myofibers—the material used to carefully construct the artwork's interior—firmly wrap around my hand, allowing me to wield this tool as if it were a part of me.
The injections ensure an easier time collecting my ingredient, even as dozens of assailants present in the harvesting grounds pursue me.
However, mm...
A disadvantage of such a method is that it instills excitability in the user.
*huff* Emotional excitement begets impulsivity, which makes the prospects of clawing things into unrecognizable red paste...
... a rather irresistible proposition.
If only I could paint them over... with my brush...
Wouldn't that, too... make for a fine piece of art...?
What a shame.

dataList.title: The Ring Fauvist Student
dataList.content: Grrr...
If only the instruction manual specified that such a moment of passion could be redirected toward my work...
... it would have enabled the composition of... better art.
